tracks.title: Doting Basics
tracks.description: Small daily gestures that feel natural—and actually happen.

tracks.title: Deep Listening
tracks.description: Make people feel seen, understood, and safe to be real.

tracks.title: Everyday Connection
tracks.description: Notice tiny invitations and turn toward them with warmth.

tracks.title: Appreciation
tracks.description: Celebrate good news and say thank you in ways that land.

sources.title: How to Stop Detached Dating and Create Real Connection

steps.title: The Doting rule
steps.body: Better than a grand gesture: one small, specific act of care you actually do.

steps.prompt: Which is the most Doting gesture?

choices.text: Send a short message that references something they mentioned this week.
Wait until a holiday to reach out.

steps.prompt: Who do you want to nurture this week? (First name or initials)
Draft a 1–2 sentence check‑in. Make it specific, warm, and low‑pressure.
rubric: Asks a small question or makes a small offer
steps.placeholder: e.g., “Hey Sam — how did your presentation go? Want a quick coffee this week?”

steps.prompt: Gesture challenge

lessons.title: Make it easy: the if‑then plan

sources.title: How are habits formed: Modelling habit formation in the real world
Implementation Intentions and Goal Achievement: A Meta-analysis of Effects and Processes

steps.title: Good intentions need a handle
steps.body: A tiny plan beats a big promise. “If X happens, then I’ll do Y.”

steps.prompt: Which plan is most likely to happen?

choices.text: “If I pour my morning coffee, then I’ll send one check‑in.”

steps.explain: Specific cue + specific action = fewer decisions later.

steps.prompt: Pick your cue: when could your gesture naturally fit?
steps.placeholder: e.g., after lunch, on the train, when I open my laptop…

steps.prompt: Write your if‑then plan. Keep it tiny.
steps.placeholder: e.g., “If I finish lunch, then I’ll send one kind message.”

steps.prompt: Gesture challenge
steps.challenge: Set a reminder for your cue—so your plan has backup.

sources.title: Perceived Partner Responsiveness as an Organizing Construct in the Study of Intimacy and Closeness

steps.title: Specific is a form of love
steps.body: People tend to feel closer when they feel understood and cared for—specificity helps communicate that.

steps.prompt: Which message feels most responsive?

choices.text: “Thinking of you—how did your new project kickoff go?”

steps.explain: The detail signals: I noticed you. I remember you.

steps.prompt: What’s one detail you can reference?
steps.placeholder: e.g., their trip, exam, new job, sick kid, hobby…

steps.prompt: Rewrite a generic check‑in into a specific one.
steps.placeholder: e.g., “Hey—how’s your ankle feeling after the hike?”

steps.prompt: Gesture challenge

sources.title: Perceptions of Partner Responsiveness Across the Transition to Parenthood
Perceived Partner Responsiveness as an Organizing Construct in the Study of Intimacy and Closeness

steps.title: A common trap
steps.body: When someone shares stress, solutions can feel like a shortcut. Often, understanding comes first.

steps.prompt: Someone says: “Work was brutal today.” Pick the best first response.

choices.text: “You should talk to your manager tomorrow.”
“That sounds exhausting. Want to vent, want advice, or want a distraction?”

steps.explain: Name the feeling + offer options. Solutions can come later.

steps.prompt: Which phrase is most validating?

choices.text: “I get why that would bother you.”

steps.explain: Validation doesn’t mean agreement; it means understanding.

steps.prompt: Rewrite this into something validating: “You’re being dramatic.”
steps.placeholder: e.g., “This feels really big for you—tell me what part is hitting hardest.”

steps.prompt: Gesture challenge
steps.challenge: Use one validating line in a real conversation within 24 hours.

lessons.title: Mirror + label: active listening in one sentence

sources.title: Perceiving active listening activates the reward system and improves the impression of relevant experiences

steps.title: Listening isn’t silent
steps.body: Active listening includes feedback—so the other person knows you got it.

steps.prompt: Which response is the best “mirror + label”?

choices.text: “It sounds like you’re overwhelmed and tired.”

steps.explain: It reflects content + emotion without rushing to solve.

steps.prompt: A friend says: “I feel like I’m failing.” Pick the best first reply.

choices.text: “It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of pressure.”

steps.explain: Start with understanding; direction can come later.

steps.prompt: Write one mirror + label sentence for someone in your life.
rubric: Starts with “It sounds like…” or similar
steps.placeholder: e.g., “It sounds like you’re frustrated and feeling stuck.”

steps.prompt: Gesture challenge
steps.challenge: Use your sentence once this week.

sources.title: The motivational value of listening during intimate and difficult conversations

steps.title: Curiosity is a kind of care
steps.body: A single warm question can turn a moment into a conversation.

steps.prompt: Which is the most curious (and least leading) question?

choices.text: “What part of this feels most important to you?”

steps.explain: It invites meaning without putting someone on trial.

steps.prompt: Someone says: “I don’t know what I’m doing.” What question fits best?
steps.explain: Gently helps them locate what’s real.

steps.prompt: Write one curious question you can ask your focus person this week.
steps.placeholder: e.g., “What’s something you wish I understood better lately?”

steps.prompt: Gesture challenge
steps.challenge: Ask it—and then pause long enough for a real answer.

sources.title: How to Stop Detached Dating and Create Real Connection

steps.title: Tiny bids matter
steps.body: People invite connection in small ways: a comment, a sigh, a meme, a “look at this.” Noticing is half the skill.

steps.prompt: A friend texts: “This week has been a lot 😮‍💨” What is this most likely?
steps.explain: It’s an opening. Your job is to turn toward it.

steps.prompt: Pick the best “turn toward” reply:

choices.text: “Want to tell me what’s been the hardest part?”

steps.prompt: What’s one way your focus person usually “bids” for attention?
steps.placeholder: e.g., sends links, makes jokes, sighs, asks small questions…

steps.prompt: Gesture challenge
steps.challenge: Respond to one bid today with warmth + curiosity.

sources.title: How to Stop Detached Dating and Create Real Connection

steps.title: A fast reply can be a deep reply
steps.body: Turning toward can be tiny: a warm acknowledgment plus one small invitation.

steps.prompt: Which reply is best if you only have 10 seconds?

choices.text: “I saw this—thinking of you. Want to tell me more later?”

steps.explain: It acknowledges + keeps the door open.

steps.prompt: What’s a moment you often miss bids?
Write your 10‑second “turn toward” line.
steps.placeholder: e.g., “I’m in the middle of something—can I call you tonight? I care.”

steps.prompt: Gesture challenge
steps.challenge: Use your line once this week.

steps.title: Repair is a reset button
steps.body: A repair attempt is a small action or phrase that stops negativity from spiraling and helps you restart with respect.

steps.prompt: Which is the best repair line mid‑tension?

choices.text: “I’m getting sharp. Can we restart? I care about you.”

steps.explain: It names the moment and protects the relationship while you fix the issue.

steps.prompt: You hear yourself snapping. What’s the best next move?

choices.text: Make a small repair: soften tone, apologize for sharpness, propose a restart.

steps.explain: Repair doesn’t erase the problem; it protects the way you solve it.

steps.prompt: Write one repair phrase that sounds like you.
steps.placeholder: e.g., “I’m coming in hot—can I try that again more kindly?”

steps.prompt: Gesture challenge
steps.challenge: Use your repair phrase once in the next 2 weeks.

sources.title: What do you do when things go right? The intrapersonal and interpersonal benefits of sharing positive events

steps.title: Good news is connection fuel
steps.body: When someone shares a positive event, your enthusiastic engagement can deepen the moment—and the bond.

steps.prompt: Someone says: “I got the job!” What’s the best response?

choices.text: “That’s huge! Tell me what happened—how are you feeling right now?”

steps.prompt: Write your 3‑part good‑news reply (celebrate + ask + savor).
steps.placeholder: e.g., “YES!! I’m so proud of you. What part feels best? Want to celebrate this weekend?”

steps.prompt: Gesture challenge
steps.challenge: Use your reply the next time someone shares good news.

sources.title: Find, Remind, and Bind: The Functions of Gratitude in Everyday Relationships

steps.title: Specific appreciation feels real
steps.body: Gratitude can strengthen bonds when it’s concrete and personal.

steps.prompt: Which thank‑you is most likely to land?

choices.text: “Thank you for picking up dinner—it took pressure off me tonight.”

steps.prompt: Write a 3‑part gratitude note (notice + impact + value).
steps.placeholder: e.g., “When you checked on me after my meeting, it helped me breathe. I value how you show up.”

steps.prompt: Gesture challenge
